retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Oral Epithelium
retrieved_docs.content: The oral mucosa can be classified into three types that differ histologically, clinically, and functionally. The mucosa that lines the mobile structures of the mouth receives the name of lining, or moveable mucosa , which is found on the soft palate, cheeks, lips, alveolar mucosa, the floor of the mouth, and vestibular fornix. The type of epithelium that covers the lining mucosa is a non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium [1] . The rigid mucosa tightly bound to the underlying bone in the attached gingiva and hard palate is known as masticatory mucosa . The type of epithelium covering these surfaces is a keratinized or para-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium, which provides the masticatory mucosa its capacity to better support the stress upon which it is subjected during mastication. Finally, there is a specialized mucosa on the dorsum of the tongue that shows a squamous stratified epithelium that can be either keratinized or non-keratinized . It receives this name because of its unique feature of having different types of lingual papillae and taste buds that allow taste perception. Because the dorsum of the tongue actively participates in mastication, this mucosa is sometimes also classified as masticatory mucosa.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Oral Epithelium
retrieved_docs.content: The entire surface of the oral mucosa is covered by squamous stratified epithelium. This is a highly organized, avascular, and semipermeable tissue whose thickness and degree of keratinization varies according to the location in the oral cavity and the area's functional and mechanical requirements. An interdigitated interface connects the epithelium and the lamina propria. The undulating projections of the deeper layer of the epithelium, known as rete pegs , attach to the underlying papillary projections of the lamina propria. Between these two tissues, there is a non-cellular basement membrane to which the epithelium is tightly bound. The basement membrane provides support to the epithelium and connects them to the connective tissue. On light microscopy, it is seen as a line of demarcation between the epithelium and connective tissue of the lamina propria. However, it is better observed with electron microscopy that clearly shows the basal lamina, which is further subdivided into lamina lucida and lamina densa.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The oral mucosa can be classified into lining mucosa, masticatory mucosa, and specialized mucosa, with distinctive histological, clinical, and functional features. The variety of functions that the oral mucosa develops mainly includes protecting the underlying tissues from mechanical, chemical, and biological stimuli, secretion of essential substances, and a sensory function, which allows the perception of temperature, touch, pain, and taste [2] [3] [4] [2] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Histologically, the oral mucosa is formed by three layers, a surface squamous stratified epithelium, known as the oral epithelium , whose thickness and degree of keratinization depend on the location and functional requirements [1] . Beneath is an underlying connective tissue, known as lamina propria, and a dense irregular connective tissue, the submucosa, which is found at the deepest level. The latter is absent in some oral cavity regions, where the lamina propria is directly bound to the bone or muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Microscopy, Light
retrieved_docs.content: Light microscopy (LM) is used to visualize the different structures of a stained specimen oral mucosa. By understanding the normal appearance of the oral mucosa, the identification of pathological changes in the tissue is possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Submucosa
retrieved_docs.content: Ectopic sebaceous glands, known as Fordyce granules, may be found on the submucosa layer of the oral mucosa in some locations. Even though they have always been considered a normal variation, recent studies have suggested that people with increased lipid profiles have a higher number of Fordyce granules. Hence this clinical finding should not be disregarded. They are mostly found on the buccal mucosa and labial mucosa. Age and smoking reduce their density [24] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Oral Epithelium
retrieved_docs.content: Four layers form the oral epithelium in keratinized oral mucosa, which is the case of the masticatory mucosa. Commencing from the deeper layer, we found the stratum basale followed by stratum spinosum, stratum granulosum, and stratum corneum. When the epithelium is nonkeratinized, such as in the lining mucosa, above the stratum basale, there is a stratum filamentosum and stratum distendum [1] . Moreover, the non-keratinized epithelium, seen on the lining mucosa, lacks the granular layer, and the spinous layer is known to be generally thinner [5] . The cells that form the epithelium are attached to each other by desmosomes and get gradually flatten from the stratum basale upwards to the stratum corneum, where they acquired a scaly or squamous appearance. Squamous epithelial cells are known as keratinocytes since they are mostly composed of cytokeratins.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Oral Epithelium Replenishment
retrieved_docs.content: The oral epithelial cells are frequently replaced by cell division, around each 14 to 21 days. This is because the oral cavity is constantly exposed to high functional demands, which necessitate frequent turnover. The replenishment process starts at the stratum basale, mainly formed by mitotic cells that undergo proliferation first and then a differentiation and migration process [2] . The turnover is known to be faster in the movable mucosa than the masticatory one (see table 1). To maintain the homeostasis of the epithelium, there is a need for differentiation and desquamation at the surface that balances cell division. When the homeostasis gets altered by factors such as aging or pathological conditions, it may result in a hyperplastic or atrophic epithelium [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Merkel Cells
retrieved_docs.content: In the oral cavity, they are mostly located in the keratinized epithelium of the maxillary and mandibular gingivae and hard palate. However, these Merkel cell-neurite complexes are located in higher numbers in the mucosa of the lingual gingivae. Therefore, it is believed that they act as slow adapting mechanoreceptors and give somatosensory information on the tongue's position [18] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The oral mucosa has a large number of functions of clinical significance. It acts as a protective physical and immune barrier from external stimulus and harbors minor salivary glands that secret saliva maintaining the wetness of the tissue, and perceives and responds to the stimulus of temperature, touch, and pain. Multiple diseases may affect the oral mucosa and impair its capacity to develop these tasks. To mention some examples, the oral epithelium disruption can cause various oral mucosal lesions such as oral lichen planus and oral leukoplakia. Oral carcinomas, such as squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), have been developed when the oral epithelial barrier of the mucosa is disrupted and keratinocytes undergo defective differentiation [2] . Furthermore, the gingival barrier destruction caused by dysbiosis in the oral microbiota combined with inflammation leads to periodontal diseases [2] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The mucous membrane that lines the structures within the oral cavity limits is known as oral mucosa . This is a wet soft tissue membrane that extends from the junction between the vermilion border of the lips and labial mucosa anteriorly to the palatopharyngeal folds posteriorly.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Histochemistry and Cytochemistry
retrieved_docs.content: Some components of the oral mucosa are identified by immunohistochemistry techniques. For example, Langerhan cells require specific immunohistochemical reactions, such as S-100 immunohistochemistry. Merkel cells are not easily observed in samples stained with hematoxylin and eosin and are usually identified with immunohistochemistry techniques. They express simple cytokeratin intermediate filaments CK 7, 8, 18, 19, and 20. Moreover, CK20 is only found in taste buds and Merkel cells in the stratified squamous epithelium [18] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Mucosal Lesions, Immunologic Diseases -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of oral mucosal lesions involves a thorough physical examination, detailed subjective history, and tissue biopsy if indicated. A bimanual exam with adequate illumination is used to characterize the size, shape, color, texture, and location of the lesion(s). While assessing a patient, the clinician must determine the onset and duration of the lesion, the change in character or size, and the presence, absence, and quality of pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Function -- Secretion
retrieved_docs.content: The main substance secreted by the oral mucosa is saliva, which is released by the ducts of the major and minor salivary glands. The minor salivary glands widely distributed in the oral cavity are included in the submucosa. Still, the major salivary glands, which are the main source of saliva, are located outside the limits of the oral mucosa. However, their excretory ducts open into the oral cavity contributing to the maintenance of the wetness of the tissue.The oral mucosa possesses a less significant amount of sebaceous glands reported on the lips, labial, and buccal mucosa in the majority of the adult population and may be found sporadically in the alveolar mucosa. These glands secret a fatty substance, known as sebum, whose functions have not yet been determined. However, recent studies of skin sebum suggest that it may play a role in immunity [25] .

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Oral Mucosa -- Structure -- Oral Epithelium
retrieved_docs.content: The stratum basale features a layer of cuboidal or columnar cells that are above the basement membrane to which hemidesmosomes attach them. These cells are known for their mitotic capacity. Just above the stratum basale, several layers of larger cells called prickle cells, due to their shape, form the stratum spinosum. The stratum granulosum comes; next, these cells contain small cytoplasmatic keratohyalin granules that strongly stain with hematoxylin. Finally, the more superficial layer, the stratum superficiale or stratum corneum, is a keratinized layer composed of very flat cells depicted by the lack of nucleus and by staining pink with eosin [6] .

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2569
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic adenocarcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck exhibits the morphological characteristics of سرطانة غدية نقيلية, indicating that the cancerous cells in the affected lymph node display the typical features of a metastatic glandular carcinoma. This association helps pathologists recognize the tumor type and guides clinicians in diagnosing and managing metastatic disease involving head and neck lymphatic structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: are each classified as cancerous tumors and are included within the general categories of pancreatic cancer and malignant neoplasms, respectively. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical organization in which specific gastrointestinal, pancreatic, head‑and‑neck, sublingual‑gland, and cutaneous malignancies are linked to broader concepts of secondary malignancies and non‑hematologic malignant disease groups. This structure supports consistent clinical coding, precise morphological description, and comprehensive patient management across diverse cancer types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is clinically associated with head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق), indicating that smoking is a significant risk factor for the development of malignancies in the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures. Head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق) can manifest as metastasis to head and neck lymph nodes (Metastasis to head and neck lymph node), reflecting a common pattern of regional spread for these tumors. Together, these concepts underscore the importance of tobacco cessation in preventing head and neck cancer and the need for careful evaluation of cervical lymph nodes when diagnosing and staging the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1989
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids for local oral treatment and other agents for local oral treatment in ATC are both classified as stomatological preparations, indicating that each is a type of medication formulated for use within the oral cavity to manage dental or mucosal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2567
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of head and neck is recognized as a specific subtype of سرطان الخلايا الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق (head and neck squamous cell carcinoma). This metastatic disease displays the characteristic morphology associated with سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية (metastatic squamous cell carcinoma), indicating that the pathological features of the tumor in the lymph nodes reflect the typical patterns of squamous cell carcinoma spread. Understanding that this condition represents an advanced, morphologically distinct form of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2575
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck displays morphological characteristics that are associated with السرطان النقيلي, indicating that the histopathologic appearance of the metastatic tumor in cervical lymph nodes reflects the typical morphology of السرطان النقيلي and aids in accurate diagnosis and appropriate management of patients with head‑and‑neck metastatic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2667
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis to head and neck lymph node is recognized as a manifestation of head and neck cancer (سرطان الرأس والعنق). This relationship indicates that the spread of malignant cells to the lymph nodes within the head and neck region is a specific form of head and neck cancer, highlighting its relevance for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1154
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic carcinoma to lymph node of head and neck is classified as a type of الورم السرطاني (malignant tumor) and its associated morphology is described by الورم النقيلي (metastatic cancer). This indicates that the spread of carcinoma to the lymph nodes in the head and neck region represents a malignant neoplasm whose pathological features correspond to metastatic disease, a distinction that is important for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 265
retrieved_docs.content: integrating primary sites (colon, stomach, upper jaw, prostate, lung, sublingual gland, retina), metastatic destinations (lymph nodes of the neck, head and neck, sublingual gland), morphological variants, and broader disease categories (secondary malignancies, non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ). This integrated framework supports accurate diagnosis, prognostication, and site‑specific therapeutic planning for patients with diverse malignant neoplasms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1459
retrieved_docs.content: Microbiology is identified as an applicable qualifier for the oral conditions قيلة مثانية, قيلة مستقيمية, and اضطراب اللسان, indicating that microbiological factors are relevant in the assessment or management of these disorders. Bacterial vaccines are, in turn, an applicable qualifier for microbiology, reflecting the role of immunization strategies within the broader microbiological context. The term “Without” is noted as not being associated with either قيلة مثانية or قيلة مستقيمية, suggesting that the absence of a particular factor does not have a direct relationship with these specific conditions. Together, these connections highlight the importance of microbiological considerations and vaccine‑related interventions in certain oral pathologies, while clarifying that “Without” does not contribute to their etiology or presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lymph nodes of the head and neck is associated with the morphological characteristics of سرطانة الخلايا الحرشفية النقيلية, indicating that the histopathologic appearance of this disease corresponds to the Arabic term describing metastatic squamous cell carcinoma.
